One Link
How it works Features Security Share Get One Link
Mirror

Host your own copy. In five minutes.

This website is a static bundle. There is no database, no application server, no proprietary build step. Every byte lives in a public AGPL-3.0 repository. If you do not trust our host, run your own.

Why mirror

The architecture expects you to.

Resilience is not a feature we add on top. It is the default state. A network that depends on one website to function is not a network. A network that depends on one website to be discoverable is a different problem, and this page is the answer to it.

Censorship resistance.

State actors block domains. Registrars seize them. CDNs unilaterally deplatform. Hosting providers terminate. Every link in the chain that brings this page to your browser is a single point of failure. Mirroring removes it.

Trust verification.

If you can serve the same bytes we serve, signed with the same offline-held release key, you have proof we did not modify the content for your visitor specifically. Reproducibility is a defense against targeted attacks.

Latency + locality.

Communities behind slow links, in regions where Cloudflare nodes are sparse, or on networks where outbound HTTPS is filtered can host the bytes locally. Static, ~58 KB brotli, no server-side runtime.

It is yours, in every sense.

AGPL-3.0 grants you the right. The site declares the intent. The architecture invites it. If we step away tomorrow, the network keeps running on the relays you and others are already operating, and the website keeps living on the mirrors you and others are already hosting.

Three minutes

Run a local copy.

For your own use. Zero internet exposure. Python 3 only, no other dependencies.

git clone https://github.com/IamOneYouAreOneWeAreOne/one-link-website
cd one-link-website/dist/weareone-link.org
python3 -m http.server 8080

Open http://localhost:8080. The site is now live on your machine. The mesh widget will not find peers (it relies on the upstream Durable Object) but every static page renders identically, and the WASM demos all run fully in your browser.

Five minutes

Run a public mirror.

For your community. Behind a domain you own, on a VPS, Raspberry Pi, or home server. Caddy reverse-proxies and gets you a Let's Encrypt cert automatically.

sudo apt install caddy
git clone https://github.com/IamOneYouAreOneWeAreOne/one-link-website /var/www/one-link

cat > /etc/caddy/Caddyfile << 'EOF'
your-mirror-domain.example {
    root * /var/www/one-link/dist/weareone-link.org
    file_server
    encode br gzip

    header /css/*    Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable"
    header /live/*   Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable"
    header /images/* Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable"
    header /*.html   Cache-Control "public, max-age=0, must-revalidate"

    header Content-Security-Policy "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'wasm-unsafe-eval'; style-src 'self'; img-src 'self' data: blob:; font-src 'self'; connect-src 'self'; worker-src 'self'; manifest-src 'self'; media-src 'self' blob:; object-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'none'; frame-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'; form-action 'self'; upgrade-insecure-requests"
    header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload"
    header X-Content-Type-Options nosniff
    header Referrer-Policy "no-referrer"
    header Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy "require-corp"
    header Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy "same-origin"
    header Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy "same-origin"
    header Permissions-Policy "interest-cohort=(), browsing-topics=()"
}
EOF

sudo systemctl reload caddy

Caddy gets the cert automatically. Your mirror is online with the same security headers as the canonical site. Point your DNS A/AAAA records at your VPS, wait for the cert, and you are live.

Ten minutes

Onion service on Tor.

For visitors behind hostile networks, on Tor, or under surveillance pressure. The bytes are identical. The .onion address is permanent and globally routable without DNS or CAs.

sudo apt install tor

cat >> /etc/tor/torrc << 'EOF'
HiddenServiceDir /var/lib/tor/one_link_mirror/
HiddenServiceVersion 3
HiddenServicePort 80 127.0.0.1:8080
EOF

sudo systemctl reload tor
sudo cat /var/lib/tor/one_link_mirror/hostname

That last command prints your .onion address (56 characters, ends in .onion). Anyone running Tor Browser can reach your mirror at that address. No registrar, no DNS, no CA, no IP exposure. The address is the public key.

Then run a tiny local server on 127.0.0.1:8080 serving the static bundle (same python3 -m http.server 8080 as above, run as a systemd service so it survives reboots).

Free hosting paths

If you have no infrastructure.

These paths require an account on a hosting provider but no payment.

Cloudflare Pages.

Fork the repository on GitHub. In your Cloudflare dashboard, create a Pages project pointing at your fork. Build command: empty. Output directory: dist/weareone-link.org. Deploy. Your mirror is live at <project>.pages.dev within two minutes.

GitHub Pages.

Fork the repository. In repo Settings → Pages, select source “main branch / dist/weareone-link.org folder.” The Worker logic does not run on Pages, so dynamic endpoints (/api/topology, /download/<os>) return 404, but every static page including the WASM demos works fully.

Netlify.

Fork the repository. Connect Netlify to the fork. Publish directory: dist/weareone-link.org. Build command: empty. Same caveat as GitHub Pages re: dynamic endpoints. Static pages and WASM demos work.

Vercel.

Same idea. Fork, connect, set Output Directory to dist/weareone-link.org. Set Framework Preset to “Other”. Build Command empty. Same caveat re: dynamic endpoints.

Decentralized hosting

IPFS pin.

For content-addressed hosting that does not depend on any single domain or provider. Anyone with the CID can fetch the bundle through any IPFS gateway. The bytes are identical to what we serve.

ipfs add -r dist/weareone-link.org

The final line of output is the CID. Share it as ipfs://<CID> or via any HTTPS gateway like https://<CID>.ipfs.dweb.link. Pin the CID on a service like Pinata, Web3.Storage, or Filebase, or pin it on your own IPFS node, and the content stays available even if every centralized host disappears.

If you want a stable hostname, point an IPNS record or a DNSLink TXT record at the CID and update it when the content changes. The signed manifest in the bundle is the integrity proof; the CID is the addressing proof.

Verify before you serve

Make sure the bytes are real.

The site ships with a signed manifest at /manifest.json. Every asset hash is signed under the offline-held release key. Run this once after cloning to confirm the bundle has not been tampered with.

cd one-link-website
python3 scripts/verify-manifest.py

# expected output:
#   manifest signature: OK (Ed25519 over canonical bytes)
#   asset count:        32 files
#   per-asset SHA-256:  32/32 match
#   verdict:            bundle integrity verified

If verification fails, do not host the bundle. Open an issue and pull a fresh clone. The verification script is itself in the repository and itself verifiable.

Tell the world

Your mirror, on the list.

If you run a public mirror you are willing to be listed, add it via pull request to docs/MIRRORS.md in the website repository. The README will list mirrors by region. We do not endorse any specific mirror, we just publish the registry.

You are also free to mirror without listing. The point is not centralization of the mirror list, it is the existence of mirrors.

Fork the repository → Mirror registry
One Link

A free, private network for messages, files, and devices. Owned by no one. Belongs to everyone.
